title: Psalms 55
subtitle: Cast Your Cares on the LORD

sections.header: Verses 1–15 — “If an Enemy Were Insulting Me, I Could Endure It”

heb.paragraph: "If an enemy were insulting me, I could endure it... but it is you, a man like myself, my companion (mērēʿî), my close friend, with whom I once enjoyed sweet fellowship" (vv.12–14). The betrayal psalm. Not an enemy but a friend. The shared worship makes the treachery unbearable: they walked together to the house of God (v.14). David wishes he had wings like a dove to fly away (v.6). His anguish is both emotional and physical: trembling, horror, heart pounding (vv.4–5).

notes.note: "My heart is in anguish within me; the terrors of death have fallen on me. Fear and trembling have beset me. Oh, that I had the wings of a dove! I would fly away and be at rest." The flight fantasy: escape from betrayal. The dove represents innocence and freedom.

"It is not an enemy who insults me — I could bear that. But it is you, my companion, my close friend, with whom I enjoyed sweet fellowship as we walked to the house of God." The wound of betrayal by a friend is deeper than any enemy’s attack. Echoes Psalm 41:9 and Judas’ betrayal.

refs.note: Jesus overwhelmed with sorrow and troubled in Gethsemane parallels the psalm's restless anguish.
Jesus deeply distressed and troubled echoes the psalmist's trembling and horror.
Conflicts on the outside and fears within captures the psalm's description of overwhelming anxiety.

hist.context: Psalm 55 is an individual lament dealing with betrayal by a close friend amid urban violence. The wish for dove's wings to fly away (vv.6-8) expresses the desire to escape overwhelming circumstances. The city full of malice, abuse, threats, lies, and corruption (vv.9-11) suggests either Jerusalem under wicked leadership or metaphorical urban chaos.

sections.header: Verses 16–23 — “Cast Your Cares on the LORD and He Will Sustain You”

notes.note: "Evening, morning and noon I cry out, and he hears my voice." Three-times-daily prayer: David’s practice that becomes the church’s liturgical rhythm.
"Cast your cares on the LORD and he will sustain you." The therapy for betrayal: transfer the weight. "He will never let the righteous be shaken" — the stability promise from Psalm 15:5 and 16:8.

"But you, God, will bring down the wicked into the pit of decay. Bloodthirsty and deceitful will not live out half their days. But as for me, I trust in you." The psalm ends with trust amid unresolved betrayal.

refs.note: 1 Peter 5:7 alludes to or echoes the concept of Psalm 55:22 but does not directly quote it.
Jesus's teaching on anxiety develops the psalm's instruction to cast burdens on the LORD.
Betrayal by a close friend who ate bread together parallels this psalm's treacherous companion.

hist.context: The intimate betrayal described (vv.12-14, 20-21) has been connected to Ahithophel, David's counselor who joined Absalom's rebellion (2 Sam 15-17). 'My companion, my close friend, with whom I once enjoyed sweet fellowship' describes the agony of broken trust. The contrast between the friend's smooth speech and war in his heart (v.21) unmasks the betrayer's duplicity.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: One betrayed by a close companion, longing for escape (vv.1-8,12-14,16-23)
ppl.text: David wishes for 'the wings of a dove' to 'fly away and be at rest' (v.6) — one of the Psalter's most memorable images of the desire to flee suffering. But the psalm's real anguish is relational: 'If an enemy were insulting me, I could endure it… but it is you, a man like myself, my companion, my close friend' (vv.12-13). They 'once enjoyed sweet fellowship' walking to God's house together (v.14). Tradition associates this with Ahithophel's betrayal during Absalom's revolt (2 Sam 15-17), though the psalm transcends any single event. David's three-daily prayer practice (v.17) became the basis for Jewish fixed-hour prayer.

ppl.name: The Betraying Friend
ppl.role: Former intimate whose treachery is worse than open enmity (vv.12-14,20-21)
ppl.text: This unnamed companion 'attacks his friends' and 'violates his covenant' (v.20). His 'talk is smooth as butter, yet war is in his heart; his words are more soothing than oil, yet they are drawn swords' (v.21). If Ahithophel, the parallel is complete: a trusted counselor whose advice was 'like that of one who inquires of God' (2 Sam 16:23), who defected to Absalom and ultimately hanged himself (2 Sam 17:23) — a narrative arc echoed in Judas's betrayal of Jesus.

ppl.name: God (Elohim)
ppl.role: The one who hears and sustains when human trust fails (vv.16-19,22-23)
ppl.text: Against the backdrop of human betrayal, God is the one who 'saves me' (v.16) and 'hears my cry' (v.17). The instruction 'cast your cares on the LORD and he will sustain you' (v.22) is quoted directly in 1 Peter 5:7. God 'will never let the righteous be shaken' (v.22) — not a promise against suffering but against ultimate abandonment. The psalm resolves not in circumstances changing but in trust transferred from unreliable humans to the faithful God.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVListen to my prayer, O God, do not ignore my plea;ESVGive ear to my prayer, O God, and hide not yourself from my plea for mercy!NIVI would flee far away and stay in the desert;ESVyes, I would wander far away; I would lodge in the wilderness; Selah

rec.text: ""My heart is in anguish within me; the terrors of death have fallen on me. Fear and trembling have beset me. Oh, that I had the wings of a dove! I would fly away and be at rest." The flight fantasy: e…"

rows.label: Cast Your Cares — 55:16–23
rows.text: "CAST YOUR CARES on the LORD." Three-daily prayer. "He will never let the righteous be shaken."

hebtext.note: "If an enemy were insulting me, I could endure it... but it is you, a man like myself, my companion (mērēʿî), my close friend, with whom I once enjoyed sweet fellowship" (vv.12–14). The betrayal psalm. Not an enemy but a friend. The shared worship makes the treachery unbearable: they walked together to the house of God (v.14). David wishes he had wings like a dove to fly away (v.6). His anguish is both emotional and physical: trembling, horror, heart pounding (vv.4–5).
יְהָבְךָ (yəhāḇəḵā) = what he has given you / your lot / your portion

themes.note: Faith peaks: casting cares on God. Justice: betrayal by a friend is the deepest injustice. Prophecy: echoes Judas’ betrayal.

prayer_prompt: As you consider 'Cast your cares on the LORD and he will sustain you,' consider asking God which specific burden you are still carrying that He has told you to release — and what keeps you gripping it.
